title: Dyeing with horsetail
subtitle: How to use common horsetail to dye wool a creamy yellow-green, with notes on its self-mordanting properties
excerpt: Common horsetail (Equisetum arvense) produces creamy yellow-green tones on protein fibres and contains silica that assists dye uptake without a separate mordant, though alum mordanting improves the colour depth and washfastness. The dye bath exhausts well and the spent bath can be reused for lighter values.
sourceNotes: Horsetail as a natural dye plant is documented in Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour' and in Adrosko's 'Natural Dyes and Home Dyeing'. The self-mordanting properties described are consistent with the silica content documented in plant chemistry references. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A quality of certain dye plants that contain enough tannins, silica, or minerals to assist dye uptake on protein fibres without a separate mordant. Horsetail has partial self-mordanting properties due to its silica content; it can produce usable colour without alum, but alum mordanting improves depth and washfastness.

The used dye bath remaining after dyeing is complete. Depending on the dye plant and mordant, an exhaust bath can be reused for subsequent batches of fibre at reduced colour depth. Horsetail exhaust produces progressively lighter values and is useful for achieving a tonal range from one dyeing session.

Wool, silk, alpaca, and other animal-derived fibres that contain keratin or similar proteins. Protein fibres have a natural affinity for most natural dyes and mordants, making them easier to dye than cellulose fibres (cotton, linen). Most natural dye recipes are written for protein fibres unless otherwise specified.

content.text: plant, which means it can produce colour on
without a pre-mordanting step. The resulting yellow-green is softer than when alum is used. Adding alum mordanting gives a clearer and more lasting colour. The

produces progressively lighter values, useful for building a tonal range from one batch of plant material. Horsetail is common in the UK, growing in damp roadsides and grassland. The Herbal Apothecary category covers horsetail identification and its non-dyeing traditional uses.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Alum is safe at dye quantities. Use separate vessels from food preparation and dispose of dye exhaust water responsibly.

items.name: Fresh or dried horsetail stems, packed loosely
Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate), optional but recommended
Large stainless steel dye pot, at least 6 litres
Digital scales, thermometer, and long-handled spoon

content.text: Dissolve 12 g of alum in 3 litres of warm water. Add the pre-wetted wool and heat gradually to 80°C over 30 minutes. Hold for 45 minutes, then remove the wool and set aside without rinsing. To use the self-mordanting route without alum, skip this step and proceed directly to the dye bath.

Place the horsetail in 3 litres of cold water and bring slowly to 80°C. Simmer for 45 minutes until the liquid is a clear yellow-green. Strain out the plant material and return the liquid to the pot. Enter the mordanted or pre-wetted wool into the warm dye bath, heat back to 75°C, and hold for 45 minutes, stirring gently.
Step 3: Exhaust bath for lighter values

After removing the first batch of dyed wool, add a second pre-mordanted fibre to the same dye bath and repeat the 45-minute simmer at 75°C. The second batch will be noticeably lighter. A third batch from the same exhaust is possible but yields only a very pale tint. Label each skein with the exhaust number to track the tonal range.

Rinse in progressively cooler water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently and hang to dry. Alum-mordanted horsetail dye has reasonable washfastness; unmordanted results fade more quickly with washing.

items.problem: The colour is very yellow with no green component
items.cause: The horsetail was over-simmered and the green compounds broke down before the dye bath was strained
items.fix: Simmer the plant material at a lower temperature (70°C rather than 80°C) and strain promptly after 45 minutes. Keeping the extraction temperature lower preserves the green compounds that contribute to the yellow-green tone.

items.problem: The result using the self-mordanting route is much paler than expected
items.cause: The plant's natural silica content alone was insufficient for the fibre amount used
items.fix: Re-mordant the pale wool with a fresh alum bath and re-dye in a new horsetail dye bath. Alum mordanting reliably doubles the colour depth on protein fibre compared to the self-mordanting route.
